los_items.los: LOS 58a: Describe the six components of the Code of Ethics
los_items.explanation: The CFA Institute Code of Ethics establishes the ethical foundation for all CFA members and candidates. The six components require members to act with integrity, competence, diligence, and respect; place client interests before their own; practice in an ethical manner; promote the integrity of capital markets; maintain and develop professional competence; and uphold the rules governing the CFA Program. These principles define the spirit behind the more specific Standards of Professional Conduct.
key_points: Six components: integrity/competence, client priority, ethical practice, market integrity, professional development, CFA Program rules
Code of Ethics is aspirational and broad; Standards are specific and prescriptive
Both the Code and Standards are testable — know them separately
Violations of Standards are also violations of the Code
Applies to all CFA members and candidates globally

los_items.los: LOS 58b: List the seven Standards of Professional Conduct
los_items.explanation: The seven Standards of Professional Conduct provide specific rules that operationalize the Code of Ethics. They are organized as: Standard I (Professionalism), Standard II (Integrity of Capital Markets), Standard III (Duties to Clients), Standard IV (Duties to Employers), Standard V (Investment Analysis, Recommendations, and Actions), Standard VI (Conflicts of Interest), and Standard VII (Responsibilities as a CFA Institute Member or Candidate). Each standard contains sub-standards that address distinct situations.
key_points: I: Professionalism — law, independence, misrepresentation, misconduct
II: Integrity of Capital Markets — material nonpublic info, market manipulation
III: Duties to Clients — loyalty, fair dealing, suitability, performance presentation, confidentiality
IV: Duties to Employers — loyalty, additional compensation, supervisory responsibilities
V: Investment Analysis — diligence, communication, record retention
VI: Conflicts of Interest — disclosure, priority of transactions, referral fees
VII: Responsibilities as Member/Candidate — conduct, reference to CFA designation

los_items.los: LOS 59a (Standard I): Describe knowledge of the law and the obligations under Standard I(A)
los_items.explanation: Standard I(A) requires members to understand and comply with all applicable laws, rules, and regulations — including the CFA Institute Code and Standards — that govern their professional activities. When there is a conflict between local law and the Code and Standards, members must follow the stricter of the two. Members are not required to report violations but must dissociate from any ongoing violations.
key_points: Follow the stricter of local law vs. CFA Standards — never the more lenient
Dissociation is required when violations are discovered and cannot be stopped
No affirmative duty to report violations to authorities (but may be required by local law)
Ignorance of the law is not a defense — members must stay informed
Compliance procedures must be maintained to prevent violations

los_items.los: LOS 59b (Standard I): Describe the requirements of Standards I(B) through I(D) regarding independence, misrepresentation, and misconduct
los_items.explanation: Standard I(B) requires members to maintain independence and objectivity, avoiding any pressures that could compromise their professional judgment, including gifts or compensation that could influence their work. Standard I(C) prohibits misrepresentation — members must not make false statements about investment analysis, qualifications, or services. Standard I(D) prohibits conduct involving dishonesty, fraud, or deceit, as well as any act that reflects adversely on professional reputation.
key_points: I(B): Modest gifts acceptable; gifts that could compromise independence must be refused or disclosed
I(B): Research analysts must protect independence from investment banking pressure
I(C): Misrepresentation includes plagiarism and false claims about the CFA designation
I(C): Performance data must not be misrepresented — cherry-picking results is a violation
I(D): Personal conduct outside work can violate I(D) if it reflects on professional character

los_items.los: LOS 60a (Standard II): Describe the requirements of Standard II(A) regarding material nonpublic information
los_items.explanation: Standard II(A) prohibits members from trading or inducing others to trade on material nonpublic information (MNPI). Information is material if a reasonable investor would consider it important in an investment decision, and nonpublic if it has not been disseminated to the marketplace. Members who possess MNPI must not act on it until it becomes public through proper channels.
key_points: Material = reasonable investor would consider it significant to an investment decision
Nonpublic = not yet disseminated to the general market
Both trading and tipping on MNPI are prohibited
Firewall procedures between departments help prevent MNPI from crossing
Selective disclosure by companies to analysts does not make information public

los_items.los: LOS 60b (Standard II): Explain the mosaic theory and its application
los_items.explanation: The mosaic theory holds that an analyst can legally combine material public information with nonmaterial nonpublic information to reach an investment conclusion, even if that conclusion would have been material if disclosed directly. This is not a violation of Standard II(A) because neither input piece alone is both material and nonpublic. Analysts should document their research process to demonstrate that conclusions were reached through legitimate mosaic analysis.
key_points: Mosaic = material public info + nonmaterial nonpublic info = legal and permissible
The resulting conclusion can be material and acted upon — no violation
Key distinction: nonmaterial nonpublic + public info is allowed; material nonpublic is not
Document the research process to support a mosaic defense
Expert networks are acceptable under mosaic theory if information is nonmaterial

los_items.los: LOS 61a (Standard III): Describe the fiduciary duties under Standard III: duties to clients
los_items.explanation: Standard III establishes that members must always act in the best interest of their clients, placing client interests above their own or their employer's. Key sub-standards cover loyalty and prudence (III(A)), fair dealing (III(B)), suitability (III(C)), performance presentation (III(D)), and preservation of confidentiality (III(E)). The overarching fiduciary duty means client interests come first in all dealings.
key_points: III(A): Loyalty and prudence — act in client's best interest, not employer's or member's own
III(B): Fair dealing — treat all clients fairly, not necessarily equally; time-sensitive info goes to all simultaneously
III(D): Performance presentation — must not misrepresent performance; GIPS compliance is recommended
III(E): Client information is confidential unless disclosure is required by law or client consents
Soft dollar arrangements must benefit the client and be fully disclosed

los_items.los: LOS 61b (Standard III): Distinguish between discretionary and non-discretionary accounts and suitability requirements
los_items.explanation: Standard III(C) requires that before making any investment recommendation or taking action, members must determine that it is suitable for the client based on their investment profile. In discretionary accounts, the member has authority to make investment decisions, so suitability must be evaluated for each trade. In non-discretionary accounts, the client makes final decisions, but the member still must ensure recommendations are suitable.
key_points: Discretionary: member decides — suitability must be assessed for every action taken
Non-discretionary: client decides — member still must recommend only suitable investments
Suitability factors: risk tolerance, return objectives, time horizon, liquidity needs, tax situation, legal constraints
Suitability is assessed at the portfolio level, not just per security in isolation
Must update the investment policy statement (IPS) when client circumstances change

los_items.los: LOS 62a (Standard IV): Describe duties to employers under Standards IV(A) through IV(C)
los_items.explanation: Standard IV governs members' obligations to their employers. IV(A) requires loyalty — members must not act against their employer's interest, though this does not prevent them from seeking new employment. IV(B) requires disclosure and written consent before accepting additional compensation that could create a conflict of interest. IV(C) requires that supervisors take reasonable steps to detect and prevent violations by those under their supervision.
key_points: IV(A): Loyalty — cannot take clients or records when leaving; may prepare to compete while still employed
IV(B): Additional compensation must be disclosed in writing and approved by employer
IV(C): Supervisors must establish compliance procedures; cannot delegate supervisory responsibility
If a supervisor learns of violations, they must respond promptly and adequately
Having a compliance system is not enough — it must be effective and enforced

los_items.los: LOS 63a (Standard V): Describe the components of investment analysis, recommendations, and actions under Standard V
los_items.explanation: Standard V requires members to exercise diligence and thoroughness in all investment analysis and recommendations (V(A)), maintain a reasonable and adequate basis supported by research (V(A)), communicate clearly and distinguish between fact and opinion (V(B)), and keep records to support investment decisions (V(C)). Members must clearly communicate risk and limitations of their analysis.
key_points: V(A): Reasonable basis — analysis must be thorough; relying on third-party research requires due diligence
V(B): Communication — distinguish clearly between facts and opinions in client reports
V(B): Disclose risks, limitations, and key assumptions underlying recommendations
V(C): Record retention — maintain records for a minimum of seven years
Using quantitative models requires understanding their limitations and assumptions

los_items.los: LOS 64a (Standard VI): Describe the disclosure requirements under Standard VI regarding conflicts of interest
los_items.explanation: Standard VI requires members to disclose all actual and potential conflicts of interest to clients and employers so they can assess the objectivity of advice. VI(A) covers disclosure of conflicts (e.g., ownership of analyzed securities); VI(B) requires client transactions to take priority over member's personal transactions; and VI(C) requires disclosure of referral fees. Full and timely disclosure enables informed consent.
key_points: VI(A): Disclose beneficial ownership in recommended securities and other material conflicts
VI(B): Priority of transactions — client accounts first, then employer proprietary accounts, then personal
VI(B): Blackout periods before and after client trades help prevent front-running
VI(C): Referral fees must be disclosed before entering an agreement — enables clients to assess bias
Disclosure must be prominent and timely — buried footnotes may not suffice

los_items.los: LOS 65a (Standard VII): Describe the requirements of Standard VII regarding conduct as a member and GIPS
los_items.explanation: Standard VII governs members' conduct in their roles as CFA Institute members or candidates. VII(A) prohibits conduct that compromises the integrity of the CFA examination or the CFA Program. VII(B) addresses the proper use of the CFA designation — it must not be used in a misleading way, and candidates who have not passed all exams must not imply they hold the charter. GIPS compliance is referenced as a recommended standard under this framework.
key_points: VII(A): Do not cheat, share exam questions, or misrepresent the exam process
VII(B): CFA designation is an adjective, not a noun — 'CFA charterholder,' not 'a CFA'
Cannot say 'CFA Level II candidate' after failing — must accurately represent status
Expressing confidence in passing is acceptable; implying the charter has been earned is not
GIPS compliance is encouraged and referenced in Standard VII context

los_items.los: LOS 66a: Describe the key features of the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS)
los_items.explanation: GIPS are a set of voluntary, ethical standards for calculating and presenting investment performance, established by CFA Institute to ensure consistency and comparability across investment managers globally. Key features include the requirement to define composites (groupings of all fee-paying discretionary portfolios with similar strategies), use of time-weighted returns, mandatory inclusion of all actual, fee-paying discretionary portfolios in at least one composite, and presentation of at least 5 years of GIPS-compliant history (building to 10 years).
key_points: Composites must include ALL fee-paying discretionary portfolios with similar strategies — no cherry-picking
Time-weighted return (TWR) is required to eliminate the effect of external cash flows
Minimum 5 years of compliant history required initially; build to 10 years over time
Returns must be calculated after actual trading expenses (i.e., gross of management fees or net of trading costs)
GIPS apply to investment management firms, not to individual funds or portfolios

los_items.los: LOS 66b: Explain why GIPS are important and how they are administered
los_items.explanation: GIPS are important because they provide a standardized framework that allows prospective clients to compare investment managers' performance on a consistent, apples-to-apples basis, reducing the risk of misleading performance presentations. They build trust by ensuring that historical returns are not cherry-picked or manipulated. GIPS are administered by CFA Institute and are voluntary — firms self-declare compliance, though third-party verification is strongly encouraged.
key_points: Primary purpose: allow fair comparison of manager performance across firms and geographies
Compliance is voluntary but self-declared — no government mandate
Third-party verification is recommended but not required for compliance claims
Firms must claim compliance on a firm-wide basis, not just for selected products
GIPS reduce information asymmetry between managers and prospective clients

los_items.los: LOS 1a: Interpret interest rates as required rates of return, discount rates, or opportunity costs
los_items.explanation: An interest rate can be viewed from three perspectives depending on context. As a required rate of return, it is the minimum return an investor demands for a given level of risk. As a discount rate, it converts future cash flows into present value terms. As an opportunity cost, it represents the return foregone by choosing one investment over another.
key_points: Interest rates serve three equivalent interpretive roles: required return, discount rate, opportunity cost
Higher risk investments demand higher required rates of return
The opportunity cost concept underlies all TVM calculations
Discount rates are used to find present value of future cash flows
All three interpretations yield the same numerical rate — context determines the label

los_items.los: LOS 1b: Explain an interest rate as the sum of a real risk-free rate and premiums for inflation, default, liquidity, and maturity risk
los_items.explanation: The nominal interest rate on any security can be decomposed into additive components that compensate investors for different types of risk and erosion of purchasing power. The real risk-free rate is the base return in the absence of risk and inflation. Each premium — inflation, default, liquidity, and maturity — adds to this base to reflect additional compensation required by investors.
key_points: Nominal rate ≈ real risk-free rate + inflation premium + default risk premium + liquidity premium + maturity risk premium
T-bills approximate the nominal risk-free rate; TIPS approximate the real risk-free rate
Default risk premium compensates for the chance the borrower will not repay
Liquidity premium compensates for difficulty converting an asset to cash quickly
Maturity risk premium reflects greater price sensitivity of longer-duration instruments to interest rate changes

los_items.los: LOS 1c: Calculate and interpret the effective annual rate, given the stated annual interest rate and the frequency of compounding
los_items.explanation: The stated annual interest rate (also called the nominal rate or APR) does not account for intra-year compounding, so it understates the true annual return. The effective annual rate (EAR) converts any compounding frequency into an equivalent annual rate, allowing valid comparison across instruments with different compounding periods. As compounding frequency increases, EAR rises and approaches continuous compounding in the limit.
key_points: EAR = (1 + periodic rate)^m − 1, where m is the number of compounding periods per year
EAR > stated rate whenever compounding occurs more than once per year
For continuous compounding: EAR = e^r − 1
Use EAR to compare instruments with different compounding frequencies on equal footing
Periodic rate = stated annual rate / m

los_items.los: LOS 1d: Calculate the solution for time value of money problems with different compounding periods
los_items.explanation: When compounding periods differ from annual, both the interest rate and number of periods must be adjusted to match the compounding frequency. The periodic rate equals the annual rate divided by the number of compounding periods, and n is expressed in total compounding periods rather than years. Mastery of these adjustments is essential for solving mortgage, bond, and savings problems on the exam.
key_points: Adjust: i/Y = annual rate ÷ m; N = years × m
Never mix annual rates with non-annual periods — always convert consistently
Monthly compounding is the most common adjustment (mortgages, consumer loans)
Calculator inputs must match the compounding basis of the problem
Continuous compounding uses FV = PV × e^(r×n)

los_items.los: LOS 2a: Calculate and interpret the future value (FV) and present value (PV) of a single sum of money, an annuity, and a series of unequal cash flows
los_items.explanation: FV and PV calculations are the core of all TVM problems. A single sum grows by compounding or shrinks by discounting. Annuities — either ordinary (end-of-period) or annuity due (beginning-of-period) — are valued with dedicated formulas or calculator functions. Unequal cash flow series require discounting each cash flow individually and summing the results.
key_points: FV = PV × (1 + r)^n; PV = FV / (1 + r)^n
Ordinary annuity: payments at end of period; annuity due: payments at beginning (multiply ordinary result by (1+r))
Perpetuity PV = PMT / r
For unequal cash flows, use the CF and NPV functions on a financial calculator
Annuity due always produces a higher PV and FV than an ordinary annuity for the same inputs

los_items.los: LOS 2b: Demonstrate the use of a timeline in modeling and solving time value of money problems
los_items.explanation: A timeline is a visual representation that places cash flows at their correct points in time, making complex TVM problems tractable and reducing errors. Each period is marked on a horizontal axis, with inflows shown as positive values and outflows as negative values at the appropriate time node. Drawing a timeline before solving forces clarity about the timing and sign of every cash flow.
key_points: Time 0 = today; cash flows are placed at the end of each period by convention for ordinary annuities
Positive cash flows are inflows; negative cash flows are outflows
Timelines prevent sign and timing errors — always draw one for multi-period problems
Mixed streams (lump sums + annuities) are easily decomposed using a timeline
Aligning compounding periods with cash flow periods is visually confirmed on the timeline

los_items.los: LOS 7a: Calculate and interpret the net present value (NPV) and the internal rate of return (IRR) of an investment
los_items.explanation: NPV measures the total value created by an investment by summing all discounted cash flows and subtracting the initial cost; a positive NPV means the project adds value. IRR is the discount rate at which NPV equals zero — the rate of return the project earns on invested capital. Both methods rely on discounting all cash flows back to the present at an appropriate rate.
key_points: NPV = Σ CFt/(1+r)^t − Initial Investment; accept if NPV > 0
IRR is found by setting NPV = 0 and solving for r (use calculator CF/IRR functions)
NPV decision rule: accept all independent projects with NPV ≥ 0
IRR decision rule: accept if IRR ≥ required rate of return (hurdle rate)
NPV and IRR usually agree on accept/reject for independent projects but can conflict for mutually exclusive projects

los_items.los: LOS 7b: Contrast the NPV rule to the IRR rule and identify problems with the IRR rule
los_items.explanation: While NPV and IRR often yield the same accept/reject decision for independent projects, they can conflict for mutually exclusive projects because IRR ignores the scale of investment. The IRR method also has mathematical problems: it can produce multiple IRRs when cash flows change sign more than once, and it implicitly assumes reinvestment at the IRR rather than the cost of capital. NPV is theoretically superior because it directly measures value creation.
key_points: IRR problem 1 — scale: a smaller project can have higher IRR but lower NPV than a larger project
IRR problem 2 — multiple IRRs: each sign change in cash flows can produce an additional IRR
IRR problem 3 — reinvestment assumption: IRR assumes cash flows are reinvested at IRR; NPV assumes reinvestment at cost of capital (more realistic)
For mutually exclusive projects, always rely on NPV to maximize firm value
Modified IRR (MIRR) addresses the reinvestment rate problem but is less commonly tested at Level 1

los_items.los: LOS 3a: Describe the characteristics of and differences among nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio scales
los_items.explanation: Measurement scales classify data by the mathematical operations that are meaningful for each type. Nominal scales simply categorize with no ordering; ordinal scales rank but differences are not meaningful; interval scales have meaningful differences but no true zero; ratio scales have all properties including a true zero, making ratios meaningful. The scale type determines which statistical measures are appropriate.
key_points: Nominal: categories only (e.g., asset class labels); no ranking, no arithmetic
Ordinal: ranked order (e.g., credit ratings); differences between ranks are not equal
Interval: equal differences meaningful, no true zero (e.g., temperature in Celsius); ratios not meaningful
Ratio: true zero, all arithmetic valid (e.g., returns, prices, weights)
Most financial data is ratio scale — this permits mean, variance, and ratio calculations

los_items.los: LOS 3b: Calculate and interpret measures of central tendency, including mean, median, and mode
los_items.explanation: Central tendency measures describe the center of a data distribution. The arithmetic mean is the sum divided by the count and is sensitive to outliers. The median is the middle value when sorted and is robust to extreme values. The mode is the most frequently occurring value and may not be unique. For skewed distributions, these three measures diverge in a predictable way.
key_points: Arithmetic mean: simple average; used for single-period returns and most averaging tasks
Geometric mean: used for multi-period compound returns; always ≤ arithmetic mean
Median is preferred over mean when the distribution is skewed or contains outliers
Positively skewed: mean > median > mode; negatively skewed: mean < median < mode
Weighted mean is used for portfolio returns: Rp = Σ wi × Ri

los_items.los: LOS 3c: Calculate and interpret measures of dispersion, including range, variance, and standard deviation
los_items.explanation: Dispersion measures quantify the spread of data around its center. Range is the simplest measure but uses only two data points. Variance averages squared deviations from the mean and is always non-negative, while standard deviation is its square root, expressed in the same units as the data. The sample variance uses n−1 in the denominator (Bessel's correction) to produce an unbiased estimate of population variance.
key_points: Range = Maximum − Minimum; simple but ignores all interior data
Population variance: σ² = Σ(xi − μ)² / N; sample variance: s² = Σ(xi − x̄)² / (n−1)
Standard deviation is the square root of variance and is more interpretable (same units as data)
Mean absolute deviation (MAD) = Σ|xi − x̄| / n; always less than or equal to standard deviation
A higher standard deviation indicates greater dispersion and risk in a financial context

los_items.los: LOS 3d: Calculate and interpret the coefficient of variation and Sharpe ratio
los_items.explanation: Both the coefficient of variation (CV) and Sharpe ratio are standardized risk measures that allow comparison across assets or portfolios with different means and units. CV expresses risk per unit of return and is useful when comparing investments with different mean returns. The Sharpe ratio adjusts excess return (above the risk-free rate) by standard deviation, making it the most widely used risk-adjusted performance measure.
key_points: CV = Standard Deviation / Mean; lower CV = better risk per unit of return
CV is unitless and allows comparison across assets with different return levels
Sharpe Ratio = (Rp − Rf) / σp; higher is better
Sharpe ratio penalizes total volatility (both upside and downside), which is a known limitation
Roy's Safety-First Ratio = (E[Rp] − RL) / σp, where RL is the minimum acceptable return threshold

los_items.los: LOS 5a: Describe a normal distribution and the empirical rule
los_items.explanation: The normal distribution is a continuous, symmetric, bell-shaped distribution fully described by its mean and variance. The empirical rule states that approximately 68%, 95%, and 99.7% of observations fall within one, two, and three standard deviations of the mean, respectively. Its symmetry means skewness equals zero and the mean, median, and mode are all equal.
key_points: Normal distribution: symmetric, mean = median = mode, skewness = 0, excess kurtosis = 0
A normal distribution is completely described by μ and σ²
Lognormal distributions (prices) are right-skewed; their logarithms are normally distributed
Standard normal (Z) distribution has μ = 0, σ = 1; Z = (X − μ) / σ

los_items.los: LOS 5b: Calculate probabilities for normally distributed random variables
los_items.explanation: To find probabilities for a normal random variable, it is standardized to a Z-score using Z = (X − μ) / σ, then the standard normal table (or calculator) is used to find the cumulative probability. The symmetry of the normal distribution allows calculation of tail probabilities and interval probabilities using complementary relationships. Confidence intervals for population parameters use critical Z-values.
key_points: Standardize: Z = (X − μ) / σ; this converts any normal variable to the standard normal
P(X ≤ x) = Φ(Z); P(X > x) = 1 − Φ(Z)
Key critical values: Z = 1.645 (90% CI), 1.960 (95% CI), 2.576 (99% CI)
Interval probability: P(a ≤ X ≤ b) = Φ(Z_b) − Φ(Z_a)
Confidence interval: x̄ ± Z_(α/2) × (σ / √n)

los_items.los: LOS 4a: Define a random variable, an outcome, and an event
los_items.explanation: A random variable is a variable whose value is determined by a chance process, such as the return on a stock. An outcome is a single possible realization of a random variable — one specific value from the sample space. An event is a set of one or more outcomes, and probabilities are assigned to events. This vocabulary is foundational to all probability calculations on the exam.
key_points: Random variable: quantity whose value is uncertain and governed by a probability distribution
Outcome: a single possible result of one trial (e.g., a specific portfolio return)
Event: a collection of outcomes; may be simple (one outcome) or compound (multiple outcomes)
Sample space: the complete set of all possible outcomes
Exhaustive and mutually exclusive events partition the sample space completely

los_items.los: LOS 4b: Identify the two defining properties of probability, and distinguish among empirical, subjective, and a priori probabilities
los_items.explanation: Any valid probability measure must satisfy two properties: each probability must be between 0 and 1 inclusive, and the probabilities of all mutually exclusive and exhaustive events must sum to 1. Probabilities can be estimated from historical frequency (empirical), assigned based on personal judgment (subjective), or derived from logical analysis of equally likely outcomes (a priori). All three approaches produce numbers that obey the same mathematical rules.
key_points: Property 1: 0 ≤ P(E) ≤ 1 for any event E
Property 2: Σ P(Ei) = 1 for all mutually exclusive, exhaustive events
Empirical probability: relative frequency from historical data (e.g., stock market crash frequency)
A priori probability: derived from logic/symmetry (e.g., coin flip = 0.5)
Subjective probability: personal belief/judgment; not reproducible, but must still obey the two properties

los_items.los: LOS 4c: Calculate and interpret conditional probabilities using Bayes' formula
los_items.explanation: Conditional probability P(A|B) is the probability of event A given that event B has already occurred, and it equals P(A∩B)/P(B). Bayes' formula reverses the conditioning: it allows you to update a prior probability P(A) to a posterior probability P(A|B) after observing new information B. This is particularly useful in investment analysis for updating return expectations when new economic data arrives.
key_points: Bayes' formula: P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B)
P(B) can be expanded using total probability: P(B) = P(B|A)P(A) + P(B|Ac)P(Ac)
Posterior probability = likelihood × prior / marginal probability of evidence
Multiplication rule: P(A∩B) = P(A|B) × P(B)
If A and B are independent: P(A|B) = P(A), so P(A∩B) = P(A) × P(B)

los_items.los: LOS 6a: Explain hypothesis testing and the process of testing hypotheses
los_items.explanation: Hypothesis testing is a formal statistical procedure to assess whether sample data provide sufficient evidence to reject a maintained assumption (the null hypothesis) in favor of an alternative. The process begins by stating H₀ and Hₐ, choosing a significance level α, computing a test statistic from sample data, and comparing it to a critical value or p-value to make a reject/fail-to-reject decision. The significance level α represents the maximum acceptable probability of incorrectly rejecting a true null.
key_points: H₀ (null hypothesis) typically contains the equality; Hₐ (alternative) contains the inequality
Significance level α = probability of Type I error (rejecting a true H₀)
Reject H₀ when |test statistic| > critical value, or when p-value < α
Fail to reject H₀ does not mean H₀ is true — only that evidence is insufficient
One-tailed tests examine one direction; two-tailed tests examine both directions (reject in either tail)

los_items.los: LOS 6b: Identify the appropriate test statistic and interpret results for a test of the population mean
los_items.explanation: Testing a hypothesis about a population mean requires choosing between the z-statistic and t-statistic based on whether population variance is known and sample size. With known population variance, the z-test is used; with unknown variance (the common case), the t-test with n−1 degrees of freedom is appropriate. For large samples (n > 30), the t-distribution approximates the normal, so the distinction becomes less critical in practice.
key_points: Z-test: use when σ² is known OR n is large (> 30) with σ² unknown by convention
T-test: use when σ² is unknown and n is small (≤ 30); degrees of freedom = n − 1
Test statistic for mean: z (or t) = (x̄ − μ₀) / (s / √n)
T-distribution is flatter and has heavier tails than normal; converges to normal as df increases
p-value = smallest α at which H₀ would be rejected; reject H₀ if p-value < chosen α

los_items.los: LOS 6c: Distinguish between Type I and Type II errors
los_items.explanation: A Type I error occurs when a true null hypothesis is incorrectly rejected; its probability equals the significance level α, which is controlled by the researcher. A Type II error occurs when a false null hypothesis is not rejected; its probability is denoted β. The power of a test is 1 − β — the probability of correctly rejecting a false null. There is an inherent tradeoff: reducing α (fewer Type I errors) increases β (more Type II errors) for a fixed sample size.
key_points: Type I error: rejecting a TRUE H₀; probability = α (significance level)
Type II error: failing to reject a FALSE H₀; probability = β
Power of test = 1 − β; higher power is better
Tradeoff: decreasing α increases β (for fixed n); increasing n reduces both errors simultaneously
In practice, α is set first (e.g., 5%), accepting the resulting β as a consequence

los_items.los: LOS 8a: Distinguish among types of markets: goods, factor, and financial markets
los_items.explanation: Markets are categorized by what is being exchanged. Goods markets involve the buying and selling of final products and services. Factor markets involve inputs to production such as labor, capital, and land. Financial markets facilitate the exchange of financial instruments like stocks, bonds, and currencies.
key_points: Goods markets: consumers buy finished products from firms
Factor markets: firms buy inputs (labor, capital, land) — prices are wages, interest, rent
Financial markets: transfer of funds between savers and borrowers
Households supply factors and demand goods; firms supply goods and demand factors
All three market types interact in the circular flow of income model

los_items.los: LOS 8b: Explain the principles of demand and supply analysis and how prices are determined in competitive markets
los_items.explanation: Demand reflects consumers' willingness and ability to buy a good at various prices — as price rises, quantity demanded falls (law of demand). Supply reflects producers' willingness to sell at various prices — as price rises, quantity supplied rises (law of supply). Equilibrium price and quantity are determined where the demand and supply curves intersect, clearing the market.
key_points: Law of demand: inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded
Law of supply: positive relationship between price and quantity supplied
Equilibrium: Qd = Qs; price adjusts to eliminate surplus or shortage
A surplus (excess supply) causes price to fall; a shortage (excess demand) causes price to rise
Consumer surplus = area above price and below demand curve; producer surplus = area below price and above supply curve

los_items.los: LOS 8c: Describe the effects of a shift in demand or supply on equilibrium price and quantity
los_items.explanation: A shift in a curve (as opposed to movement along it) is caused by a change in a non-price determinant. When demand increases (curve shifts right), both equilibrium price and quantity rise. When supply increases (curve shifts right), equilibrium price falls and quantity rises. Simultaneous shifts require analysis of relative magnitude to determine the net effect.
key_points: Demand shifters: income, prices of substitutes/complements, tastes, expectations, number of buyers
Supply shifters: input costs, technology, prices of related goods, expectations, number of sellers
Increase in demand → higher P and higher Q
Increase in supply → lower P and higher Q
When both curves shift, one outcome (P or Q) is determinate and one is ambiguous without knowing relative magnitudes

los_items.los: LOS 9a: Calculate and interpret price, income, and cross-price elasticities of demand
los_items.explanation: Elasticity measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to changes in a given variable. Price elasticity of demand (PED) = %ΔQd / %ΔP; if |PED| > 1, demand is elastic; if < 1, it is inelastic. Income elasticity distinguishes normal goods (positive) from inferior goods (negative). Cross-price elasticity distinguishes substitutes (positive) from complements (negative).
key_points: PED = %ΔQd / %ΔP (always negative; use absolute value for interpretation)
|PED| > 1: elastic (luxury, many substitutes); |PED| < 1: inelastic (necessities, few substitutes)
Income elasticity > 0: normal good; < 0: inferior good; > 1: luxury good
Cross-price elasticity > 0: substitutes; < 0: complements
Total revenue test: elastic demand → price cut increases revenue; inelastic → price cut decreases revenue

los_items.los: LOS 9b: Describe the demand for and supply of labor, and how wages are determined
los_items.explanation: Labor demand is a derived demand — firms hire workers up to the point where the marginal revenue product of labor (MRPL) equals the wage rate. Labor supply reflects workers' trade-off between work and leisure; supply curves typically slope upward. The equilibrium wage is set where labor supply and demand intersect, and shifts in either curve change wages and employment levels.
key_points: Firms demand labor up to the point: MRPL = Wage (profit-maximizing condition)
MRPL = marginal product of labor × marginal revenue
Labor demand shifts right when product price rises or labor productivity improves
Labor supply shifts from changes in population, immigration, participation rates, or preferences
Minimum wages set above equilibrium create unemployment (surplus of labor)

los_items.los: LOS 10a: Describe the characteristics of perfect competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly, and monopoly
los_items.explanation: Market structures differ by number of firms, product differentiation, barriers to entry, and pricing power. Perfect competition has many firms, homogeneous products, and zero long-run economic profit. At the other extreme, monopoly features a single seller with high barriers and significant pricing power. Oligopoly and monopolistic competition fall in between, with varying degrees of interdependence and differentiation.
key_points: Perfect competition: price taker, P = MC = minimum ATC in long run, zero economic profit
Monopolistic competition: differentiated products, easy entry, downward-sloping demand, zero LR economic profit
Oligopoly: few large firms, high barriers, strategic interdependence (game theory applies)
Monopoly: single seller, high barriers, P > MC, deadweight loss, positive economic profit
Only perfect competition achieves allocative efficiency (P = MC) and productive efficiency (P = min ATC)

los_items.los: LOS 10b: Explain price discrimination and its welfare effects
los_items.explanation: Price discrimination occurs when a firm charges different prices to different customers for the same good, based on their willingness to pay. First-degree (perfect) price discrimination extracts all consumer surplus as producer surplus. Third-degree discrimination (most common) charges different prices to identifiable groups. Price discrimination increases output relative to simple monopoly, reducing but not eliminating deadweight loss.
key_points: Requires: market power, ability to identify segments, and ability to prevent resale (arbitrage)
1st degree: unique price per unit — producer captures all surplus, no deadweight loss
2nd degree: price varies by quantity purchased (e.g., bulk discounts)
3rd degree: different prices to different groups (students, seniors) based on elasticity differences
Groups with more inelastic demand are charged higher prices; total output increases vs. single-price monopoly

los_items.los: LOS 11a: Describe the components of GDP using the expenditure and income approaches
los_items.explanation: GDP measures the total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given period. The expenditure approach sums GDP = C + I + G + (X − M), where each component represents a spending category. The income approach sums all incomes earned in production (wages, rents, interest, profits). Both approaches yield the same GDP figure by the circular flow identity.
key_points: Expenditure approach: GDP = C + I + G + (X − M); net exports = X − M
C = personal consumption; I = gross private investment (includes inventory changes); G = government spending (not transfers); X − M = net exports
Income approach: GDP = wages + rent + interest + profit + indirect taxes − subsidies
Nominal GDP uses current prices; Real GDP adjusts for price level changes using a base-year index
GDP deflator = (Nominal GDP / Real GDP) × 100; broader measure than CPI

los_items.los: LOS 11c: Describe the phases of the business cycle and their characteristics
los_items.explanation: The business cycle describes recurring expansions and contractions in economic activity. The four phases are expansion (rising output, employment, and inflation), peak (maximum output), contraction/recession (falling output, rising unemployment), and trough (minimum output). Understanding cycle phases helps analysts forecast the impact on corporate earnings, interest rates, and asset prices.
key_points: Expansion: GDP rising, unemployment falling, inflation rising, consumer confidence high
Peak: GDP at cyclical high, capacity constraints, inflation at cycle high
Contraction/recession: GDP falling for two consecutive quarters, unemployment rising
Trough: GDP at cyclical low, unemployment at cycle high, deflation risk
Leading indicators (e.g., stock prices, building permits) predict turning points; lagging indicators (e.g., unemployment rate) confirm them

los_items.los: LOS 11b: Compare the monetary and fiscal policy tools available to governments
los_items.explanation: Monetary policy is conducted by the central bank and primarily targets price stability and employment through controlling the money supply and interest rates. Fiscal policy is enacted by the government through changes in spending and taxation to influence aggregate demand. Both policies can be expansionary (to stimulate growth) or contractionary (to reduce inflation), but differ in speed, flexibility, and transmission mechanisms.
key_points: Monetary tools: open market operations (most common), reserve requirements, discount/policy rate, quantitative easing
Expansionary monetary policy: buy securities / lower rates → increase money supply → lower borrowing costs → more investment and consumption
Fiscal tools: government spending changes and tax changes
Expansionary fiscal policy: increase G or cut taxes → increase aggregate demand (subject to crowding-out)
Fiscal policy lags (recognition, legislative, implementation) are typically longer than monetary policy lags; monetary policy acts faster but has uncertain transmission

los_items.los: LOS 12a: Describe how exchange rates are quoted and calculate cross-exchange rates
los_items.explanation: An exchange rate is the price of one currency in terms of another. The price currency is the one being quoted per unit of the base currency (e.g., USD/EUR means USD is the price currency and EUR is the base). A cross rate is an exchange rate derived from two other exchange rates when a direct quote is unavailable. Consistency in currency conventions is essential for accurate calculations.
key_points: Exchange rate quote: price currency / base currency (the base is always '1 unit')
Direct quote (from domestic perspective): domestic currency per foreign currency unit
Indirect quote: foreign currency per domestic currency unit
Cross rate: A/C = (A/B) × (B/C) — multiply through ensuring the intermediate currency cancels
Appreciation of a currency means it buys more foreign currency; depreciation means it buys less

los_items.los: LOS 12b: Describe the carry trade and calculate its profit
los_items.explanation: A carry trade involves borrowing in a low-interest-rate currency and investing the proceeds in a higher-interest-rate currency to earn the interest rate differential. The trade is profitable as long as the higher-yielding currency does not depreciate by more than the interest rate spread. Carry trades are subject to sudden, large losses when exchange rates move abruptly (crash risk).
key_points: Strategy: borrow low-yield currency, convert to high-yield currency, invest, then repay
Profit ≈ interest rate differential − (depreciation of high-yield currency against low-yield currency)
If high-yield currency depreciates by more than the spread, the trade generates a loss
Carry trades violate uncovered interest rate parity in the short run (empirical puzzle)
Risk: sudden unwinding causes rapid appreciation of funding currency (carry crash)

los_items.los: LOS 12c: Explain absolute and relative purchasing power parity
los_items.explanation: Purchasing power parity (PPP) is based on the law of one price — identical goods should cost the same in different countries when expressed in the same currency. Absolute PPP states that the exchange rate equals the ratio of domestic to foreign price levels. Relative PPP states that the percentage change in the exchange rate equals the inflation differential between countries; it is more useful in practice.
key_points: Absolute PPP: S = P_domestic / P_foreign (exchange rate = ratio of price levels)
Relative PPP: %ΔS ≈ inflation_domestic − inflation_foreign
If domestic inflation > foreign inflation, domestic currency depreciates accordingly under relative PPP
PPP holds better over the long run; short-run deviations are common due to non-tradable goods and capital flows
The Big Mac Index is a popular informal test of absolute PPP

los_items.los: LOS 12d: Describe the factors that cause exchange rates to change
los_items.explanation: Exchange rates fluctuate due to changes in macroeconomic fundamentals and market expectations. Higher relative inflation erodes a currency's value; higher relative interest rates attract capital inflows, appreciating the currency. Current account deficits (excess imports) create selling pressure on the domestic currency, while strong growth and favorable terms of trade support it.
key_points: Higher domestic inflation → currency depreciation (relative PPP mechanism)
Higher domestic real interest rates → capital inflows → currency appreciation
Current account deficit: outflow of domestic currency to pay for imports → depreciation pressure
Strong domestic growth increases import demand, potentially weakening the currency
Political instability, risk aversion, and capital controls can also cause sharp exchange rate moves

los_items.los: LOS 13a: Describe the benefits and costs of international trade
los_items.explanation: International trade allows countries to consume beyond their domestic production frontier by specializing in goods where they have a comparative advantage and trading for the rest. Benefits include lower consumer prices, greater variety, technology transfer, and economies of scale. Costs include displacement of domestic industries, structural unemployment in import-competing sectors, and potential over-reliance on foreign suppliers.
key_points: Benefits: lower prices for consumers, greater product variety, economies of scale, technology and knowledge transfer
Costs: job losses in import-competing industries, adjustment costs, possible exploitation of low-wage workers
Trade creates net welfare gains but distributional effects — some groups lose even when the overall economy gains
Trade barriers (tariffs, quotas) protect specific industries but reduce overall economic efficiency
Tariffs raise domestic prices, generate government revenue, and create deadweight loss; quotas have similar effects but benefit quota holders instead of the government

los_items.los: LOS 13b: Distinguish between comparative and absolute advantage
los_items.explanation: Absolute advantage means a country can produce a good using fewer resources than another country. Comparative advantage means a country can produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another country. Trade is beneficial whenever comparative advantages differ, even if one country has an absolute advantage in all goods — this is the foundation of free trade theory.
key_points: Absolute advantage: produces more output per unit of input (or same output with less input)
Comparative advantage: lower opportunity cost of production relative to the trading partner
A country should export goods in which it has a comparative advantage and import goods in which it has a comparative disadvantage
Even if one country is more efficient at everything, both countries gain from trade via comparative advantage
Opportunity cost is the key metric: compare the ratio of goods each country gives up to produce one more unit

los_items.los: LOS 21a: Describe the roles of financial reporting and financial statement analysis
los_items.explanation: Financial reporting is the process by which companies communicate their financial position and performance to external parties through standardized statements. Financial statement analysis involves using those reports to evaluate a company's past performance, current condition, and future prospects. Analysts use FSA to make investment, credit, and business decisions. The goal is to transform raw financial data into actionable insights.
key_points: Financial reporting serves preparers; financial statement analysis serves users (investors, creditors, analysts).
Primary users include equity investors, creditors, and management.
FSA is backward-looking (historical) but used for forward-looking decisions.
Analysts adjust reported figures for comparability across firms and periods.
Key outputs of FSA: assessment of profitability, liquidity, solvency, and efficiency.

los_items.los: LOS 21b: Identify the financial statements and supplementary information provided in financial reports
los_items.explanation: A complete financial report includes four core statements: the income statement, balance sheet, statement of cash flows, and statement of changes in equity. These are accompanied by footnotes and supplementary disclosures that provide critical context. The Management Discussion & Analysis (MD&A) offers management's perspective on results and risks. Auditor's reports and proxy statements are also part of the broader reporting package.
key_points: Four core financial statements: income statement, balance sheet, cash flow statement, statement of changes in equity.
Footnotes are essential — they disclose accounting policies, estimates, and contingencies.
MD&A is unaudited but provides qualitative insight into results, risks, and liquidity.
Auditor's report provides an independent opinion on whether statements are fairly presented.
Supplementary schedules (e.g., segment data) enhance comparability and detail.

los_items.los: LOS 22a: Describe the general features of financial statements under IFRS and US GAAP
los_items.explanation: Both IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards) and US GAAP require financial statements to present a true and fair view of a company's financial position. IFRS is principles-based and allows more judgment, while US GAAP is rules-based and more prescriptive. Both frameworks require consistency, accrual accounting, and the going concern assumption. Key differences exist in areas like inventory costing, asset revaluation, and revenue recognition.
key_points: IFRS is principles-based; US GAAP is rules-based — IFRS allows more management judgment.
Both require accrual basis accounting and the going concern assumption.
IFRS prohibits LIFO inventory; US GAAP allows LIFO.
IFRS permits upward revaluation of PP&E and intangibles; US GAAP does not.
Convergence efforts have reduced but not eliminated differences between the two frameworks.

los_items.los: LOS 22b: Describe useful attributes of financial reports, including understandability, timeliness, and comparability
los_items.explanation: The IASB's conceptual framework identifies qualitative characteristics that make financial information useful. Fundamental characteristics are relevance (including materiality) and faithful representation. Enhancing characteristics include comparability, verifiability, timeliness, and understandability. These attributes help users make informed decisions from financial reports.
key_points: Two fundamental qualitative characteristics: relevance and faithful representation.
Enhancing characteristics: comparability, verifiability, timeliness, understandability.
Faithful representation means complete, neutral, and free from error — not necessarily accurate.
Comparability requires consistent accounting policies across periods and firms.
Timeliness means information is available before it loses its capacity to influence decisions.

los_items.los: LOS 23a: Describe the components of the income statement and calculate comprehensive income
los_items.explanation: The income statement reports revenues, expenses, gains, and losses over a reporting period, culminating in net income. Comprehensive income extends net income to include other comprehensive income (OCI) items — unrealized gains/losses on certain securities, foreign currency translation adjustments, and pension liability adjustments. EPS (basic and diluted) is reported on the face of the income statement for public companies. The multi-step format separates operating from non-operating items, which is more useful for analysis.
key_points: Comprehensive income = Net income + Other Comprehensive Income (OCI).
OCI items bypass the income statement but affect equity directly.
Common OCI items: unrealized gains/losses on AFS securities, foreign currency translation, pension adjustments.
Basic EPS = (Net Income − Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Shares Outstanding.
Diluted EPS includes dilutive effects of options, warrants, and convertible securities — always ≤ basic EPS.

los_items.los: LOS 23b: Describe the components of the balance sheet and their relationships
los_items.explanation: The balance sheet (statement of financial position) presents a company's assets, liabilities, and equity at a point in time, governed by the accounting equation: Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Assets are resources controlled by the entity expected to provide future economic benefits, while liabilities are present obligations. Equity represents the residual interest after deducting liabilities from assets. Items are classified as current (due within one year) or non-current.
key_points: Fundamental equation: Assets = Liabilities + Equity — must always balance.
Current assets/liabilities: expected to be settled within one operating cycle or 12 months.
Common current assets: cash, receivables, inventory, prepaid expenses.
Equity components: common stock, additional paid-in capital, retained earnings, treasury stock, AOCI.
Book value of equity ≠ market value; analysts often adjust for off-balance-sheet items.

los_items.los: LOS 23c: Describe the components of the statement of cash flows and their classification
los_items.explanation: The cash flow statement reconciles the change in cash and cash equivalents over a period by classifying cash flows into three activities: operating, investing, and financing. Operating activities relate to the core business operations and can be reported using the direct or indirect method. Investing activities reflect capital expenditures and acquisitions. Financing activities include debt issuance/repayment and equity transactions.
key_points: Three sections: Operating (CFO), Investing (CFI), Financing (CFF) — must know what goes where.
Indirect method starts with net income and adjusts for non-cash items and working capital changes.
Direct method shows actual cash receipts and payments — preferred by analysts but rarely used.
Under US GAAP, interest paid is CFO; under IFRS, it can be CFO or CFF.
Under US GAAP, dividends received is CFO; under IFRS, it can be CFO or CFI.

los_items.los: LOS 24a: Describe common financial ratios and their interpretation
los_items.explanation: Financial ratios are calculated from financial statement data to evaluate different dimensions of a company's performance and health. The major categories are liquidity (ability to meet short-term obligations), solvency (long-term debt capacity), profitability (efficiency of profit generation), activity/efficiency (asset utilization), and valuation (market-based metrics). Ratios are most useful when compared to industry benchmarks, historical trends, or peers.
key_points: Five ratio categories: liquidity, solvency, profitability, activity (efficiency), and valuation.
Ratios must be interpreted in context — a high current ratio may signal poor asset management.
Cross-sectional analysis compares ratios across firms; time-series analysis tracks trends over time.
Ratios can be distorted by accounting choices (e.g., LIFO vs. FIFO affects inventory and COGS ratios).
No single ratio tells the full story — use multiple ratios together for a comprehensive view.

los_items.los: LOS 24b: Calculate and interpret activity, liquidity, solvency, profitability, and valuation ratios
los_items.explanation: Liquidity ratios (current ratio, quick ratio) measure short-term solvency. Solvency ratios (debt-to-equity, interest coverage) assess long-term financial risk. Profitability ratios (ROE, ROA, net profit margin) gauge earnings efficiency. Activity ratios (asset turnover, inventory turnover, days sales outstanding) measure how effectively assets are used. These ratios together paint a complete picture of financial health.
key_points: Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities; Quick Ratio excludes inventory.
ROA = Net Income / Average Total Assets; ROE = Net Income / Average Equity.
Asset Turnover = Revenue / Average Total Assets — higher is better (more efficient use of assets).
Debt-to-Equity = Total Debt / Total Equity — higher signals more financial leverage and risk.
Interest Coverage = EBIT / Interest Expense — measures ability to service debt from operations.

los_items.los: LOS 24c: Describe the DuPont analysis of return on equity
los_items.explanation: DuPont analysis decomposes ROE into component drivers to identify the source of profitability. The classic 3-factor model breaks ROE into net profit margin, asset turnover, and the equity multiplier (financial leverage). The extended 5-factor model further separates the tax burden, interest burden, and EBIT margin. This framework allows analysts to diagnose whether ROE is driven by operational efficiency, asset utilization, or leverage.
key_points: 3-factor DuPont: ROE = Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier.
Equity Multiplier = Total Assets / Total Equity — captures financial leverage.
5-factor DuPont: ROE = Tax Burden × Interest Burden × EBIT Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier.
High ROE driven by high leverage is riskier than high ROE driven by strong margins.
DuPont is a high-frequency exam topic — memorize all components and their formulas.

los_items.los: LOS 25a: Describe the effects of accounting choices on financial ratios
los_items.explanation: Management's accounting choices — such as inventory costing method, depreciation method, and revenue recognition timing — can significantly affect reported financials and ratios. Analysts must understand these choices to make valid comparisons across firms. For example, LIFO in an inflationary environment results in higher COGS and lower net income, affecting profitability and inventory ratios. Adjusting for these differences is a key analytical skill.
key_points: LIFO (rising prices) → higher COGS, lower net income, lower inventory on balance sheet vs. FIFO.
Accelerated depreciation → higher early expenses, lower net income, lower asset values.
Capitalizing vs. expensing costs: capitalizing boosts current income but increases assets and future depreciation.
Revenue recognition timing affects reported revenue and receivables — earlier recognition inflates current period metrics.
Analysts adjust reported numbers (e.g., using LIFO reserve) to achieve comparability.

los_items.los: LOS 25b: Explain the FIFO, LIFO, and weighted-average cost methods
los_items.explanation: Inventory costing methods determine how the cost of goods sold (COGS) and ending inventory are calculated. FIFO (First-In, First-Out) assumes the oldest inventory is sold first, resulting in lower COGS and higher ending inventory in rising price environments. LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) assumes the newest inventory is sold first — allowed under US GAAP but prohibited under IFRS. Weighted-average cost smooths out price fluctuations by averaging all inventory costs.
key_points: IFRS prohibits LIFO; US GAAP allows all three methods — a critical exam distinction.
In rising prices: FIFO → lower COGS, higher net income, higher inventory; LIFO → opposite.
LIFO reserve = FIFO inventory − LIFO inventory; used to convert LIFO statements to FIFO basis.
Weighted-average cost: COGS per unit = Total Cost of Goods Available / Total Units Available.
LIFO liquidation occurs when inventory quantities decline, releasing older low-cost layers into COGS.

los_items.los: LOS 26a: Describe the treatment of long-lived assets under IFRS and US GAAP
los_items.explanation: Long-lived (non-current) assets include property, plant & equipment (PP&E), intangible assets, and investment properties. Under IFRS, companies may use the cost model or revaluation model for PP&E and intangibles with finite lives; US GAAP only permits the cost model. Goodwill from business combinations is not amortized under either framework but must be tested for impairment annually. Research costs are expensed; development costs may be capitalized under IFRS.
key_points: IFRS allows revaluation model (upward + downward adjustments); US GAAP only allows cost model (write-downs only).
Goodwill: not amortized under IFRS or US GAAP — subject to annual impairment testing.
IFRS capitalizes development costs when feasibility is established; US GAAP expenses all R&D.
Impairment under IFRS: recoverable amount = higher of fair value less costs to sell and value in use.
US GAAP impairment is a two-step test (recoverability then measurement); IFRS is a one-step test.

los_items.los: LOS 26b: Calculate and interpret depreciation methods and their effects on financial statements
los_items.explanation: Depreciation allocates the cost of a long-lived asset over its useful life. Common methods include straight-line (equal annual charges), declining balance (accelerated — higher early charges), and units-of-production (usage-based). The choice of depreciation method affects net income, asset carrying values, and financial ratios. Accelerated methods front-load expenses, resulting in lower early-period earnings and lower asset values.
key_points: Straight-line: (Cost − Residual Value) / Useful Life — same charge each period.
Double-declining balance: 2 × (1 / Useful Life) × Book Value — ignores residual value until end.
Units-of-production: (Cost − Residual Value) × (Units Produced / Total Expected Units).
Accelerated depreciation → lower early net income, lower taxes (timing difference), higher later income.
Longer useful life or higher residual value assumptions → lower annual depreciation → higher income.

los_items.los: LOS 27a: Describe income tax accounting and calculate income tax expense
los_items.explanation: Income tax expense on the income statement is based on pre-tax accounting income, not taxable income. The difference between tax expense (based on accounting income) and taxes payable (based on taxable income) is recorded as a deferred tax asset or liability. Tax expense = taxes payable + change in deferred tax liability − change in deferred tax asset. Understanding this distinction is critical because taxable income can differ significantly from accounting income.
key_points: Income tax expense ≠ taxes payable — the difference creates deferred tax assets/liabilities on the balance sheet.
Effective tax rate = Income Tax Expense / Pre-tax Income — may differ from statutory rate.
Deferred Tax Liability (DTL): taxes owed in the future — taxable income < accounting income currently.
Deferred Tax Asset (DTA): taxes paid now but recognized later — taxable income > accounting income currently.
Tax expense = Current tax expense + Deferred tax expense (change in net DTL).

los_items.los: LOS 27b: Distinguish between temporary and permanent differences in income tax
los_items.explanation: Temporary differences arise when income or expenses are recognized in different periods for accounting versus tax purposes, creating deferred tax assets or liabilities that will reverse in the future. Permanent differences are items recognized for accounting purposes but never for tax (or vice versa), and they do not create deferred tax items. Examples of permanent differences include tax-exempt municipal bond interest and non-deductible penalties. Temporary differences are more analytically significant because they affect future cash taxes.
key_points: Temporary differences reverse over time and create DTAs or DTLs on the balance sheet.
Permanent differences never reverse — they cause the effective tax rate to differ from the statutory rate.
Common temporary difference: accelerated depreciation for tax vs. straight-line for accounting → creates DTL.
DTA example: warranty expense recognized for accounting before deductible for tax purposes.
A valuation allowance reduces a DTA when it is 'more likely than not' that the benefit won't be realized.

los_items.los: LOS 28a: Distinguish between operating and finance leases and their financial statement effects
los_items.explanation: A finance lease (formerly capital lease) transfers substantially all risks and rewards of ownership to the lessee; the lessee recognizes both a right-of-use asset and a lease liability on the balance sheet. An operating lease was historically kept off-balance-sheet, but under current IFRS 16 and ASC 842, lessees must recognize a right-of-use asset and liability for most leases. Finance leases result in higher reported assets and liabilities, higher early-period expenses (front-loaded depreciation + interest), and lower early net income versus operating leases.
key_points: Under current standards (IFRS 16 / ASC 842), virtually all leases are on-balance-sheet for lessees.
Finance lease: lessee records asset + liability; expense = depreciation + interest (front-loaded).
Operating lease: expense is straight-line rent expense; still creates ROU asset + lease liability under new rules.
Finance lease → higher assets, higher liabilities, higher D/E ratio, lower current ratio vs. operating lease.
Finance lease → lower operating income initially (interest is below EBIT line) but higher CFO (principal repayment is CFF).

los_items.los: LOS 17a: Describe the types of dividends and share repurchases and how they affect shareholders
los_items.explanation: Companies return capital to shareholders through dividends (cash, stock, or special) and share repurchases. Cash dividends reduce assets and equity on the balance sheet, while stock dividends redistribute equity without changing total value. Share repurchases reduce shares outstanding, potentially increasing EPS and share price, and offer more tax flexibility than dividends.
key_points: Cash dividend: reduces cash and retained earnings; share price drops by dividend amount on ex-dividend date
Stock dividend: increases shares outstanding but reduces price proportionally — no net wealth change
Share repurchase: treated as an alternative to cash dividends; reduces shares outstanding and can signal undervaluation
Repurchases offer tax advantage when capital gains rates are lower than dividend tax rates
Special dividends are one-time payments, often used when excess cash is non-recurring

los_items.los: LOS 17b: Compare dividend policies and explain their effect on shareholder value
los_items.explanation: Dividend policy refers to how a firm decides the amount and timing of dividend payments. The three main policies are stable dividend policy, constant payout ratio, and residual dividend policy. Under M&M's dividend irrelevance theory, in perfect markets dividend policy does not affect firm value; in practice, signaling effects and investor preferences matter.
key_points: Stable dividend policy: fixed or slowly growing dividend — preferred by income investors
Constant payout ratio: dividends vary with earnings — creates unpredictability
Residual dividend policy: pay dividends only after all positive-NPV projects are funded
M&M dividend irrelevance: in perfect markets, investors can create 'homemade dividends' by selling shares
Dividend signaling: increasing dividends signals management confidence; cutting dividends signals financial stress

los_items.los: LOS 20a: Describe the key elements of a business model
los_items.explanation: A business model describes how a company creates, delivers, and captures value. Key elements include the value proposition (what problem is solved and for whom), the pricing model, the channel strategy, and the cost structure. Understanding a company's business model is essential for assessing its competitive advantage and sustainability.
key_points: Value proposition: the product or service benefit that attracts customers
Revenue model: how the firm earns money (subscription, transaction, licensing, etc.)
Cost structure: fixed vs. variable costs determine operating leverage
Channel strategy: direct vs. indirect distribution affects margins and reach
Network effects and switching costs are key sources of durable competitive advantage

los_items.los: LOS 20b: Explain the roles of stakeholders and describe corporate governance mechanisms
los_items.explanation: Corporate governance is the system of rules and practices by which a company is directed and controlled, balancing the interests of stakeholders such as shareholders, creditors, management, employees, and regulators. The principal-agent problem arises when managers (agents) act in their own interest rather than shareholders' (principals). Governance mechanisms are designed to align these interests.
key_points: Principal-agent conflict: shareholders vs. management; bondholders vs. shareholders
Board of directors is the primary internal governance mechanism — independent directors are key
External mechanisms include auditors, regulators, activist investors, and takeover threats
Compensation structures (stock options, clawbacks) align management incentives with shareholders
ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) factors are increasingly relevant to governance assessments

los_items.los: LOS 16a: Calculate and interpret the net present value (NPV) and IRR of capital projects
los_items.explanation: NPV is the present value of all future cash flows minus the initial investment, discounted at the cost of capital. A positive NPV means the project adds value and should be accepted. IRR is the discount rate that sets NPV to zero; accept a project if IRR exceeds the required rate of return. NPV is the preferred method because it directly measures value creation.
key_points: NPV > 0: accept the project; NPV < 0: reject; NPV = 0: indifferent
IRR rule: accept if IRR > required rate of return (hurdle rate)
NPV and IRR can conflict for mutually exclusive projects — always rely on NPV
IRR assumes reinvestment at the IRR rate; NPV assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital (more realistic)
Multiple IRRs can exist when cash flows change sign more than once (non-normal cash flows)

los_items.los: LOS 16b: Explain the factors affecting capital allocation decisions
los_items.explanation: Capital allocation involves deciding how to deploy a firm's financial resources among competing projects. Key factors include the project's NPV, strategic fit, risk profile, resource constraints, and management's ability to execute. Capital rationing occurs when a firm has limited funds and must prioritize projects by profitability index.
key_points: Profitability Index (PI) = PV of future cash flows / Initial investment — useful under capital rationing
Sunk costs are irrelevant; only incremental (marginal) cash flows matter
Opportunity costs must be included as a cost of the project
Externalities (cannibalization, synergies) affect incremental cash flow estimates
Real options (option to expand, abandon, delay) add value beyond standard NPV analysis

los_items.los: LOS 14a: Describe the objective of capital structure and explain the factors that influence it
los_items.explanation: Capital structure refers to the mix of debt and equity a firm uses to finance its assets. The objective is to minimize the weighted average cost of capital (WACC) and thereby maximize firm value. Key factors influencing capital structure include business risk, tax environment, financial flexibility, agency costs, and industry norms.
key_points: Optimal capital structure minimizes WACC and maximizes firm value
Higher debt increases financial risk (volatility of earnings to equity holders) but provides tax shields
Business risk (operating risk) and financial risk are both components of total firm risk
Firms with stable, predictable cash flows can support higher debt levels
Agency costs of debt (asset substitution, underinvestment) increase cost of borrowing

los_items.los: LOS 14b: Explain the Modigliani-Miller propositions with and without taxes
los_items.explanation: Modigliani and Miller (M&M) showed that in perfect capital markets (no taxes, no bankruptcy costs, no information asymmetry), capital structure is irrelevant to firm value — Proposition I. With taxes, debt creates a tax shield on interest payments, increasing firm value with leverage — Proposition I with taxes. Proposition II states that cost of equity rises with leverage to offset the cheaper cost of debt.
key_points: M&M without taxes: firm value is independent of capital structure (capital structure irrelevance)
M&M with taxes: firm value increases with debt due to interest tax shield (VL = VU + t×D)
Proposition II: re = r0 + (r0 - rd) × (D/E); cost of equity rises linearly with leverage
With taxes only, optimal structure is 100% debt — unrealistic because of bankruptcy costs
Trade-off theory: optimal leverage balances tax shield benefits against financial distress costs

los_items.los: LOS 14c: Describe target capital structure and explain factors that affect it
los_items.explanation: A target capital structure is the long-run optimal mix of debt, equity, and preferred stock that a firm aims to maintain. While firms may deviate in the short run due to market conditions or financing opportunities, they tend to revert toward the target over time. The target is influenced by the firm's tax rate, asset tangibility, profitability, and growth opportunities.
key_points: Target capital structure is used in WACC calculation as the weights (wd, we, wp)
Use market values — not book values — for WACC weights when possible
Pecking order theory: firms prefer internal financing, then debt, then equity (least preferred)
High-growth firms tend to use less debt (preserve financial flexibility)
Asset-heavy industries tend to carry more debt (tangible collateral reduces lender risk)

los_items.los: LOS 15a: Calculate and interpret the weighted average cost of capital (WACC)
los_items.explanation: WACC is the blended required rate of return for a firm, weighted by the proportion of each capital source. It represents the minimum return a project must earn to create value. WACC is used as the discount rate in NPV calculations and as a benchmark for investment decisions.
key_points: WACC = wd×rd×(1−t) + we×re + wp×rp
Weights should be based on market values, not book values
Cost of equity is estimated using CAPM: re = rf + β×(rm − rf)
Cost of preferred stock: rp = Dp / P0 (no tax adjustment since preferred dividends are not tax-deductible)
A project's discount rate should match its own risk, not the firm's WACC, if risk differs

los_items.los: LOS 15b: Explain the effect of taxes on the cost of debt and cost of equity
los_items.explanation: Interest on debt is tax-deductible, so the after-tax cost of debt is rd×(1−t), which is lower than the pre-tax rate. Dividends paid to equity holders are not tax-deductible, so the cost of equity has no tax adjustment. This tax asymmetry makes debt a cheaper source of financing and creates the interest tax shield.
key_points: After-tax cost of debt = rd × (1 − marginal tax rate)
Cost of equity is unaffected by taxes at the corporate level — no deduction for dividends
Higher tax rates increase the tax shield benefit of debt, encouraging more leverage
Use marginal (not average) tax rate for WACC calculations
For new debt, use the yield to maturity (YTM) of existing debt as a proxy for rd

los_items.los: LOS 19a: Describe the types of leverage and calculate and interpret operating, financial, and total leverage
los_items.explanation: Operating leverage measures sensitivity of operating income to changes in sales, arising from fixed operating costs. Financial leverage measures sensitivity of EPS to changes in operating income, arising from fixed financing costs (interest). Total leverage (combined) captures the overall sensitivity of EPS to sales changes.
key_points: DOL = %ΔOperating Income / %ΔSales = (Sales − Variable Costs) / Operating Income
DFL = %ΔEPS / %ΔOperating Income = EBIT / (EBIT − Interest)
DTL = DOL × DFL = %ΔEPS / %ΔSales
Higher fixed costs → higher DOL → greater operating risk
Break-even quantity Q = Fixed Costs / (Price − Variable Cost per unit)

los_items.los: LOS 19b: Explain the relationship between leverage and the risk and return characteristics of a company
los_items.explanation: Higher operating leverage magnifies both gains and losses from changes in revenue, increasing earnings volatility. Financial leverage similarly amplifies the effect of operating income changes on returns to equity holders. While leverage can enhance returns in good times, it increases the probability of financial distress in downturns.
key_points: High DOL means a larger proportion of fixed costs — earnings are more volatile relative to sales
High DFL means a larger proportion of fixed financing costs — EPS is more volatile relative to EBIT
Leverage increases expected return to equity but also increases risk (beta)
Firms with high business risk (high DOL) typically use less financial leverage (low DFL) to manage total risk
Financial distress costs (direct: legal fees; indirect: lost customers/suppliers) limit optimal leverage

los_items.los: LOS 18a: Describe working capital and calculate the cash conversion cycle
los_items.explanation: Working capital is the difference between current assets and current liabilities and represents the short-term liquidity available to a firm. The cash conversion cycle (CCC) measures how long it takes a company to convert its investments in inventory and receivables into cash, net of the time it takes to pay suppliers. A shorter CCC indicates more efficient working capital management.
key_points: Net Working Capital (NWC) = Current Assets − Current Liabilities
CCC = DSO + DOH − DPO
DSO (Days Sales Outstanding) = Accounts Receivable / (Revenue / 365) — time to collect receivables
DOH (Days of Inventory on Hand) = Inventory / (COGS / 365) — time inventory is held
DPO (Days Payable Outstanding) = Accounts Payable / (COGS / 365) — time to pay suppliers; longer is better

los_items.los: LOS 18b: Describe methods for managing a company's working capital
